```{=latex}
\appendix
```

# Feature Inventory

The tables below catalogue every feature type implemented in the feature
registry (`src/trading_rl/features/`). The subset used in the main
state-space experiment is marked with [x] in the
**In model** column; this subset is defined in
`src/configs/features/hft_lob_features_causal.yaml`.
Feature names match the keys produced by the feature pipeline at runtime.
Formulas for the active LOB features are given in Chapter 4.

All features used in the main HFT experiment are z-score normalized with causal
running statistics that are updated chronologically and reset at detected
session boundaries, with two exceptions noted below the tables: the cyclical
time encodings, which are inherently bounded in $[-1, 1]$, and the runtime
position feature, which is already bounded in $[-1, 1]$ by the action space
definition.

## LOB Microstructure Features

| Feature name | Group | Param | In model | Literature |
|---|---|---|:---:|---|
| `hft_book_pressure_l0` | Imbalance | level 0 | [x] | Cao et al. (2004); Biais et al. (1995) |
| `hft_book_pressure_l1` | Imbalance | level 1 | [x] | Cao et al. (2004) |
| `hft_book_pressure_l2` | Imbalance | level 2 | [x] | Cao et al. (2004) |
| `hft_order_book_imbalance_3l` | Imbalance | $N=3$ | [x] | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_order_count_imbalance_l0` | Imbalance | level 0 | [x] | Biais et al. (1995) |
| `hft_order_count_imbalance_l1` | Imbalance | level 1 | | Biais et al. (1995) |
| `hft_microprice` | Fair value | --- | [x] | Stoikov (2018) |
| `hft_microprice_divergence` | Fair value | --- | [x] | Stoikov (2018) |
| `hft_vwmp_skew` | Fair value | $N=3$ | [x] | Stoikov (2018) |
| `hft_price_vamp_1k` | Fair value | \$1k | | Volume-adjusted mid price |
| `hft_price_vamp_5k` | Fair value | \$5k | | Volume-adjusted mid price |
| `hft_depth_ratio` | Depth | $N=4$ | | Bouchaud et al. (2002) |
| `hft_spread_bps` | Spread/cost | --- | [x] | Kyle (1985); Glosten & Milgrom (1985) |
| `hft_spread_ratio_50` | Spread/cost | $W=50$ | [x] | Easley et al. (2012) |
| `hft_spread_ratio_200` | Spread/cost | $W=200$ | | Easley et al. (2012) |
| `hft_bid_convexity` | Spread/cost | bid side | [x] | Bouchaud et al. (2002) |
| `hft_ask_convexity` | Spread/cost | ask side | [x] | Bouchaud et al. (2002) |
| `hft_bid_slope` | Spread/cost | 5 levels | [x] | Bouchaud et al. (2002); Kyle (1985) |
| `hft_ask_slope` | Spread/cost | 5 levels | [x] | Bouchaud et al. (2002); Kyle (1985) |
| `hft_ofi` | Order flow | --- | [x] | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_ofi_rolling_10` | Order flow | $W=10$ | | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_ofi_rolling_50` | Order flow | $W=50$ | [x] | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_ofi_rolling_200` | Order flow | $W=200$ | | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_ofi_multilevel_3l` | Order flow | $N=3$ | | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_ofi_multilevel_5l` | Order flow | $N=5$ | | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_bid_queue_depletion` | Order flow | bid side | [x] | Foucault et al. (2005) |
| `hft_ask_queue_depletion` | Order flow | ask side | [x] | Foucault et al. (2005) |
| `hft_signed_trade_flow_50` | Order flow | $W=50$ | [x] | Lee & Ready (1991) |
| `hft_signed_trade_flow_200` | Order flow | $W=200$ | | Lee & Ready (1991) |
| `hft_odd_lot_trade_ratio_200` | Order flow | $W=200$ | [x] | Boehmer et al. (2021) |
| `hft_odd_lot_imbalance_200` | Order flow | $W=200$ | [x] | Boehmer et al. (2021) |
| `hft_vpin_100` | Order flow | $W=100$ | | Easley et al. (2012) |
| `hft_vpin_500` | Order flow | $W=500$ | | Easley et al. (2012) |
| `hft_cancel_to_trade_200` | Order flow | $W=200$ | | Foucault et al. (2005) |
| `hft_cancel_to_trade_1000` | Order flow | $W=1000$ | | Foucault et al. (2005) |
| `hft_trade_arrival_rate_100` | Order flow | $W=100$ | | Engle (2000) |
| `hft_trade_arrival_rate_500` | Order flow | $W=500$ | | Engle (2000) |
| `hft_large_trade_ratio_200` | Order flow | $W=200$ | | Institutional flow proxy |
| `hft_ofi_autocorr_{50,200}` | Order flow | $W \in \{50, 200\}$ | | Cont et al. (2014) |
| `hft_inter_event_time` | Regime/temp. | --- | [x] | Engle (2000) |
| `hft_mid_price_acceleration` | Regime/temp. | --- | [x] | Abergel et al. (2016) |
| `hft_hour_sin` | Regime/temp. | --- | [x] | Wood et al. (1985) |
| `hft_hour_cos` | Regime/temp. | --- | [x] | Wood et al. (1985) |

: LOB microstructure features implemented in the feature registry. The In model column marks features used in the main state-space experiment. {#tbl-lob-features longtable=true tbl-colwidths="[34,15,10,9,32]"}

Literature: the cited work that motivates or introduces the feature; not a claim that the exact implementation follows the cited paper's specification.

---

# TD3 Hyperparameters

The table below lists all hyperparameters for the main AAPL state-space
experiment. The full configuration file is
`src/configs/scenarios/aapl/td3_hft_lob_state_space.yaml`.

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | | |
| Actor hidden dims | [128, 64] | ReLU activations |
| Critic hidden dims | [128, 64] | ReLU activations |
| **Optimization** | | |
| Actor learning rate | 0.0001 | Adam |
| Critic learning rate | 0.0001 | Adam |
| Actor weight decay | 0.000002 | L2 regularization |
| Critic weight decay | 0.000002 | |
| Loss function | Smooth L1 (Huber) | |
| **TD3 mechanisms** | | |
| Discount factor $\gamma$ | 0.995 | |
| Target update $\tau$ | 0.001 | Soft update |
| Policy delay | 2 | Critic updates per actor update |
| Target policy noise $\sigma$ | 0.2 | Added to target action |
| Noise clip | 0.3 | |
| Exploration noise std | 0.1 | During training only |
| **Training** | | |
| Total steps | 2,000,000 | |
| Initial random steps | 1,000 | Warm-up before learning |
| Frames per batch | 200 | |
| Optimisation steps per batch | 5 | |
| Mini-batch size | 128 | |
| Replay buffer size | 100,000 | |
| **Data** | | |
| Training rows | 15,000 | Chronological split |
| Reward type | Differential Sharpe | $\eta = 0.01$ |
| Transaction fee | 0.000 | Baseline setting |
| Random seed | 42 | |

: TD3 hyperparameters for the main AAPL state-space experiment. Full configuration: `src/configs/scenarios/aapl/td3_hft_lob_state_space.yaml`. {#tbl-td3-hyperparams longtable=true tbl-colwidths="[36,18,46]"}

---

# Experiment Configuration Example

The YAML snippet below is a condensed version of the main experiment
configuration, illustrating the configuration-driven workflow described in
Chapter 5.

```yaml
seed: 42

data:
  data_path: "stocks/AAPL_2026-02-25_2026-02-27_raw_mbp-10_us_hours.parquet"
  feature_config: "src/configs/features/hft_lob_features_causal.yaml"
  train_size: 15000

env:
  backend: "tradingenv"
  mode: "hft"
  trading_fees: 0.000
  price_column: "close"
  reward_type: "differential_sharpe"
  reward_eta: 0.01

network:
  actor_hidden_dims: [128, 64]
  value_hidden_dims: [128, 64]

training:
  algorithm: "TD3"
  actor_lr: 0.0001
  value_lr: 0.0001
  gamma: 0.995
  tau: 0.001
  policy_noise: 0.2
  noise_clip: 0.3
  policy_delay: 2
  exploration_noise_std: 0.1
  max_steps: 2000000
  sample_size: 128
  buffer_size: 100000
  loss_function: "smooth_l1"
```

---

# TD3 Training Workflow Diagram

The diagram below summarizes the configuration-driven workflow described in
Chapter 5.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {
  'theme': 'base',
  'themeVariables': {
    'fontSize': '11px',
    'primaryColor': '#f5f4ff',
    'primaryBorderColor': '#a78bfa',
    'lineColor': '#6b7280'
  },
  'flowchart': {
    'nodeSpacing': 18,
    'rankSpacing': 20,
    'padding': 4,
    'useMaxWidth': false
  }
}}%%
flowchart TD
    A[Scenario + feature config] --> B[Pre-run validation]
    B --> C[build_prepared_dataset]
    C --> D[PreparedDataset<br/>train / val / test + metadata]
    D --> E[build_training_bundle]
    E --> F[TrainingBundle<br/>train env + trainer + callback]
    F --> G[Train TD3]
    G --> H[Checkpoint + MLflow log]
    H --> I[evaluate_all_splits]
    I --> J[Resolve primary split<br/>test -> val -> train]
    J --> K[Primary-split explainability]
    K --> L[Metrics + robustness outputs]
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# TD3 Update Mechanics Diagram

The diagram below summarizes the core TD3 update mechanics used in the thesis
implementation, abstracting away from project-specific plumbing.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {
  'theme': 'base',
  'themeVariables': {
    'fontSize': '10px',
    'primaryColor': '#f5f4ff',
    'primaryBorderColor': '#a78bfa',
    'lineColor': '#6b7280'
  },
  'flowchart': {
    'nodeSpacing': 18,
    'rankSpacing': 18,
    'padding': 4,
    'useMaxWidth': false
  }
}}%%
{{< include ./diagrams/td3_update.mmd >}}
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# RL Algorithm Taxonomy

The diagram below maps the reinforcement learning landscape from the Bellman
equation through the key algorithmic relaxations, showing where TD3 sits
relative to other families of methods. Source: author's own diagram, based on
@Sutton2018.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-width: 6.4
%%| fig-height: 8.1
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '8px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 10, 'rankSpacing': 14, 'padding': 3, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
graph TD
    A[MDP] -->|Solution| B(Bellman Equation)
    B --> C{Known model?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Dynamic Programming]
    C -->|No| E[Reinforcement Learning]
    E --> F{Value estimation?}
    F -->|Full episode| G[Monte Carlo]
    F -->|Bootstrap| H[Temporal Difference<br/>SARSA / Q-Learning]
    E --> I{State space?}
    I -->|Small / Discrete| J[Tabular Methods]
    I -->|Large / Continuous| K[Function Approximation<br/>Deep RL]
    K --> L{Action space?}
    L -->|Discrete| M[Value-Based<br/>DQN / Rainbow]
    L -->|Continuous| N[Policy-Based<br/>REINFORCE / PPO]
    L -->|Either| O[Actor-Critic]
    O --> O1{On/Off-Policy?}
    O1 -->|On-Policy| O2[A2C / PPO]
    O1 -->|Off-Policy| O3[DDPG / TD3 / SAC]
    O3 --> O4{Policy type?}
    O4 -->|Deterministic| O5[DDPG]
    O4 -->|Twin critics +<br/>delayed updates| O6[TD3 — This Thesis]
    O4 -->|Max entropy| O7[SAC]
    E --> P{Sample efficiency?}
    P -->|Need planning| Q[Model-Based RL<br/>Dyna-Q / Dreamer]
    P -->|Model-free OK| R[Model-Free RL]
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# TD3 Network Architecture

The diagram below shows how the actor, twin critics, and replay buffer interact
in the TD3 architecture used here.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-width: 5
%%| fig-height: 3.5
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '9px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 12, 'rankSpacing': 16, 'padding': 3, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
graph TD
    S[State s] --> A1[Actor mu_theta]
    A1 --> A2[Action a]
    A2 --> N1[Gaussian Noise added]
    N1 --> A3[Noisy Action]
    S --> C1[Critic Q_phi1]
    S --> C2[Critic Q_phi2]
    A2 --> C1
    A2 --> C2
    C1 --> MIN[min]
    C2 --> MIN
    MIN --> T1[Target Value]
    RB[Replay Buffer<br/>100k transitions] --> C1
    RB --> C2
    RB --> A1
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# Data Preparation Pipeline

The diagram below shows the feature pipeline design that ensures no information
from the validation or test splits leaks into the fitted normalization
parameters.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-width: 3.5
%%| fig-height: 6.8
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '8px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 10, 'rankSpacing': 14, 'padding': 3, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
{{< include ./diagrams/data_pipeline.mmd >}}
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

---

# Offline Feature Selection Pipeline

The feature selection procedure described in Section 4.2 operates in two stages:
theory constrains the candidate pool to mechanisms grounded in market
microstructure literature; IC/ICIR ranking selects among the available
implementations of those mechanisms and removes redundancy via a conditional IC
filter. This appendix describes the operational pipeline that implements Stage 2
of that procedure. It takes a theory-defined candidate pool as input, scores each
feature against a forward return proxy, and writes the selected feature
specification to a YAML file that the training pipeline loads directly.

## Feature Group Registry

Features are organized into named groups that mirror the state-space design in
Section 4.2. Each group corresponds to a distinct microstructure mechanism:

| Group | Description | Features |
|---|---|:---:|
| `imbalance` | Order book imbalance signals | 5 |
| `fair_value` | Fair value and microprice estimates | 3 |
| `spread` | Spread and cost features | 6 |
| `flow` | Order flow and trade flow features | 7 |
| `regime` | Regime and temporal features | 4 |

: Feature groups defined in the group registry (`src/configs/features/feature_groups.yaml`). The imbalance, fair value, spread, and flow groups correspond to the four signal categories in Section 4.2; the regime group provides temporal context. {#tbl-feature-groups}

The group registry is a YAML configuration that lists the full specification
(name, type, parameters, normalization flag, domain tag) for each feature within
each group. Groups can be composed by name, and individual features can be
excluded, without editing the feature source code.

## Selection Procedure

The offline selection procedure takes a candidate pool of features, scores
each one against a proxy target, and applies a greedy redundancy filter to
produce a reduced set. The procedure is run once before training; the resulting
feature list is written to a YAML file that the training pipeline loads
directly. This ensures that feature selection is not repeated on every training
run and that the reduced specification is reproducible.

**Step 1: Theory-constrained candidate pool construction.** The user specifies
which feature groups to include. Each group in `feature_groups.yaml` corresponds
to a distinct microstructure mechanism (see Section 4.2); only groups with a
theoretical prior are eligible for inclusion. The `FeatureGroupResolver` expands
group names into a flat list of `FeatureConfig` instances. Individual features
can be excluded at this stage, but no feature from outside the theory-defined
groups can be added. This constraint is enforced by the group registry structure,
not by post-hoc filtering.

**Step 2: Proxy target.** Each feature is scored against a forward log-return
target at horizon $h$:
$$y_t = \log \frac{p_{t+h}}{p_t}$$
This proxy is a cheap, model-free measure of directional predictability; it is
not the reward function used during training, but it provides a consistent
benchmark for comparing feature informativeness.

**Step 3: Scoring.** Each feature is evaluated using the Information Coefficient
(IC) framework standard in quantitative factor research. The IC of feature $f_i$
at time $t$ is its Spearman rank correlation with the forward return target:
$$\text{IC}_i(t) = \rho_{\text{Sp}}\!\left(f_i(t),\, y(t)\right)$$
computed over rolling windows on the validation split. The primary ranking
criterion is the Information Coefficient Information Ratio (ICIR):
$$\text{ICIR}_i = \frac{\overline{\text{IC}}_i}{\sigma(\text{IC}_i)}$$
where $\overline{\text{IC}}_i$ is the mean IC and $\sigma(\text{IC}_i)$ is its
standard deviation. ICIR captures both signal strength (the numerator) and
signal consistency across time (the denominator); a feature with a high mean IC
but erratic sign is penalized relative to one with a modest but stable IC.
Features with $|\text{ICIR}_i| < 0.02$ are removed before the redundancy step.
The t-statistic $t_i = \overline{\text{IC}}_i / (\sigma(\text{IC}_i) / \sqrt{n})$
is reported alongside ICIR as a measure of statistical significance.

**Step 4: Redundancy filter.** Features are ranked by ICIR. Starting from the
highest-ranked, each candidate's residual signal is evaluated: the linear
component attributable to already-selected features is regressed out of the
candidate, and its ICIR is recomputed on the residuals. A candidate is included
in the selected set only if it retains meaningful incremental predictability
after conditioning on the features already chosen. This conditional IC approach
is a principled alternative to pairwise correlation thresholding: two features
can be highly correlated yet both informative if each predicts a distinct
component of the forward return.

**Step 5: Output.** The selected feature specifications are written to a YAML
file that can be referenced directly by the training scenario configuration
via `data.feature_config`. The training pipeline reads this file at runtime;
no selection logic runs during training.

The diagram below summarizes the offline feature selection pipeline.

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
```

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-width: 5.5
%%| fig-height: 7.5
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '8px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 12, 'rankSpacing': 16, 'padding': 4, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart TD
    GRP["feature_groups.yaml<br/>(5 named groups)"]
    RES["FeatureGroupResolver<br/>expand group names"]
    POOL["Candidate Pool<br/>(25 features)"]

    GRP --> RES --> POOL

    POOL --> FIT["Fit Pipeline<br/>on Training Split"]
    FIT --> TRANS["Transform<br/>Train + Validation"]
    TRANS --> SCORE["Score Features<br/>ICIR_i = mean(IC_i) / std(IC_i)"]
    SCORE --> RANK["Rank by ICIR<br/>drop if |ICIR| < 0.02"]
    RANK --> FILTER["Conditional IC Filter<br/>residualize on selected set"]
    FILTER --> SEL["Selected Features<br/>(top_k = 12)"]
    SEL --> YAML["selected_features.yaml"]
    YAML --> TRAIN["Training Pipeline<br/>data.feature_config"]

    style GRP fill:#e1f5fe
    style SEL fill:#e8f5e9
    style YAML fill:#e8f5e9
    style TRAIN fill:#fff3e0
    style SCORE fill:#fff3e0
    style FILTER fill:#fce4ec
```

```{=latex}
\end{center}
```

The offline nature of this pipeline is deliberate: the proxy target and scoring
metrics are not the reward function that the agent optimizes during training.
Running selection as a separate step ensures that the agent's training loop
remains uncontaminated by any pre-training statistical analysis that could
introduce look-ahead bias or circular reasoning.
